questions.prompt: Which problem is fine-tuning a good fit for?

choices.text: Teaching a model new facts.
Locking in a consistent brand voice or output format using 200+ examples, especially when you want to use a shorter inference-time prompt or a smaller faster base model.
Adding tool-use capability.
Making the model fundamentally smarter at reasoning.

questions.explanation: Fine-tuning is for narrow stylistic and format behavior — voice, tone, structured output schemas. It compresses what you already do with prompting into a faster, cheaper model. It's NOT for facts (use RAG), reasoning (use a stronger or reasoning model), or tools (use function calling).

questions.prompt: You want a smaller cheaper model to match GPT-5's behavior on a narrow task. What pattern is best?

choices.text: Cross your fingers and switch models.
Distillation: log GPT-5's outputs on representative inputs, fine-tune a mini-tier model on those (input, output) pairs, evaluate. The small model learns to imitate the big one on your specific task.
Switch to a reasoning model.
Train a model from scratch.

questions.explanation: Distillation is the standard pattern. Use the strong model to generate training pairs, then fine-tune a smaller model on them. OpenAI's stored completions / fine-tuning workflow is built for this. Routinely cuts costs 5–10× on narrow tasks with no measurable quality loss.

choices.text: A token used to authenticate to the reasoning API.

A hidden token generated by a reasoning model as part of its private chain of thought. You pay for them (at the output rate) but never see them. They're what makes reasoning models slower and more expensive but stronger on hard problems.
A free bonus token.
An embedding vector.

questions.explanation: Reasoning models are trained to 'think' before answering. The thinking happens as reasoning tokens — structured, efficient, and invisible to your app. They're billed at the output token rate, which is why reasoning model bills can run away if you're not watching usage metadata.

questions.prompt: 10% of your chat traffic involves complex multi-step reasoning. The other 90% is simple. What's the right pattern?

choices.text: Send everything to a reasoning model at `high` effort.

Selective routing: a small classifier detects 'hard' queries and routes them to a reasoning model; easy queries go to GPT-5-mini. You get quality where it's needed without paying reasoning costs on the 90% of easy turns.
Send everything to GPT-5-mini.
Disable the 10% of hard queries.

questions.explanation: Selective routing is the standard production pattern for reasoning. A tiny classification step picks which route. Most traffic stays cheap; only the hard cases incur reasoning cost. Typical savings: 60–80% vs. always-on reasoning with no quality loss.

questions.prompt: What's the key risk of computer-use agents that operate real software?

choices.text: They're too fast for humans to follow.

They can take real, sometimes irreversible actions on systems they don't fully understand. Always sandbox (VM or headless browser), gate destructive actions behind approval, set hard step limits, and eval against real workflows before trusting them.
They use too few tokens.
They only work on dark-mode UIs.

questions.explanation: Computer-use agents are powerful and uniquely risky. The agent sees a screenshot and decides where to click — small UI changes can confuse it, and a confused agent in a real production system can do real damage. Sandboxing, approval gates, audit logs, and per-task evals are non-negotiable.

questions.prompt: Why is the Realtime API specifically designed for voice agents?

choices.text: It's the cheapest voice option.

Bidirectional streaming with sub-second latency including model processing, plus tool use mid-call and interrupt handling. Sequential STT → LLM → TTS introduces 2–4 second pauses that break interactive voice; Realtime is built to avoid that.
It's the only API that supports tool calling.
It auto-translates languages.

questions.explanation: Latency is the product in voice. Sequential pipelines work for one-shot audio (transcribe an upload, generate a clip) but feel broken in real-time conversation. The Realtime API streams audio in and out with sub-second response, supports interruption, and handles state — exactly what voice products need.

questions.prompt: A teammate suggests skipping the eval suite to ship faster. What's the right pushback?

choices.text: Agree — evals slow things down.

Push back. Shipping without evals means every prompt change is a leap of faith, regressions ship silently, and incidents are debugged by complaint instead of by signal. Evals are the equivalent of unit tests for AI; teams that skip them spend more time on incidents than they save on initial speed.
Agree if there's a deadline.
Skip evals but add more logging.

questions.explanation: Evals are the single highest-leverage practice in production AI. They turn shipping from vibes-based to evidence-based. The teams that maintain velocity on AI products do so because they have evals catching regressions; the teams that bog down ship-fix-ship-fix do so because they don't.

questions.prompt: Of these, which is the most important to ship with a brand-new AI feature?

choices.text: The most expensive model available.

A feature flag with one-click disable. AI features will go wrong in ways you didn't predict; the difference between a 5-minute rollback and a 5-hour rollback is whether you can flip a flag without a deploy.
A marketing landing page.
Fine-tuned models for every use case.

questions.explanation: Feature flags + gradual rollouts (1% → 10% → 100%) + one-click disable is the safety net for any new AI feature. Combined with eval suites, daily spend caps, and approval gates on risky tools, this gives you the ability to ship boldly and roll back fast — the two things AI engineering needs most.
